title: The Oscars Are Removing Four Categories From the Telecast. Why? Plus a Conversation With Nominee Nicholas Britell | The Oscars Show (Ep. 126)
format: Sean and Amanda break down a very British BAFTAs night, the Academy's plan to cut four categories from the Oscar telecast, and the screenplay races. Then composer Nicholas Britell joins to talk scoring Moonlight, Beale Street, and Succession.

segments.heading: The BAFTAs
segments.note: a very British night at the British Oscars

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Olivia Colman won Best Actress, one of the night's few breaks from the established Oscar frontrunners.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Rachel Weisz won Best Supporting Actress, historically one of the hardest BAFTA categories to read as an Oscar signal.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: Rami Malek's Best Actor win is starting to crystallize a race the hosts say they've been through the seven stages of grief with; it's nominally a film about British people and Freddie Mercury's Live Aid legacy.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Won Best Film, which the hosts read as the clearest sign yet that it's the Oscar frontrunner, even without a Golden Globes-eligible run to point to.

films.title: Lion
films.note: The precedent for how often BAFTA rewards its own: Dev Patel won an acting BAFTA for it in 2016, a performance that got little traction stateside.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: Last year's inevitable Best Picture winner, the comparison point for whether Roma's frontrunner status is as locked.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: Last year's BAFTA Best Film upset, despite being written and directed by a British filmmaker (Martin McDonagh) and being nominally about America.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: This year's "problematic fave" spoiler to Roma, alongside Bohemian Rhapsody.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: One of last year's beloved also-rans the hosts admit they tried to "secret into existence."

films.title: Dunkirk
films.note: Another of last year's crowded field of hopefuls that lost out to The Shape of Water.

films.title: Phantom Thread
films.note: Same crowded, ultimately unsuccessful field from last year.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Same, one of last year's internet favorites that came up short.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: One of this year's spoilers alongside BlacKkKlansman and A Star Is Born.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Same, part of this year's spoiler field to Roma.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Same, the film Sean is hoping squeaks out a Best Picture win.

films.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
films.note: Sean and Amanda's guess for Kate Middleton's favorite film of 2018, in a bit riffing on the royals' awkward, silent entrance at the ceremony.

segments.heading: Stock Up, Stock Down: Bradley Cooper's Campaign

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Sean Penn published a full-throated Deadline essay defending the film and Bradley Cooper's direction after Cooper was snubbed for Best Director; Paul Schrader wrote a similar note to IndieWire. The hosts are skeptical the late campaign will move voters.

films.title: The Man Who Killed Hitler and Then the Bigfoot
films.note: The Sam Elliott vehicle he's doing press for this week, which the hosts fold into their case that Elliott doesn't need an extra Best Supporting Actor push. Everyone already loves him.

segments.heading: Stock Up, Stock Down: The Oscar Telecast
segments.note: cinematography, editing, live action short, and makeup and hairstyling get cut from the broadcast

films.title: Vice
films.note: The hosts' go-to example of why the show shouldn't cut makeup and hairstyling: "there is no Vice without makeup and hairstyling."

films.title: Titanic
films.note: Cited as proof cutting categories isn't necessary to draw a huge audience: it won Best Picture (and gave out Best Live Action Short) in front of 55 million viewers.

segments.heading: The Big Race: Best Original Screenplay

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Nominee (Deborah Davis and Tony McNamara) and the favorite, one of the more inventive, thoughtful, less mechanical movies of the season.

films.title: First Reformed
films.note: Nominee, Paul Schrader, the hosts' shared "problematic fave."

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Nominee (Nick Vallelonga, Brian Currie, Peter Farrelly), and the hosts' pick to upset The Favourite.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Nominee, Alfonso Cuarón.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Nominee, Adam McKay.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Jordan Peele's win last year, the hosts' example of this category as a consolation prize: it went on to win nothing else that season.

films.title: Lost in Translation
films.note: Sofia Coppola's win, cited as "my queen" proof of the category's taste.

films.title: Thelma & Louise
films.note: Callie Khouri's win, one of the category's classic picks the hosts rattle off.

films.title: The Piano
films.note: Jane Campion's win, same rundown.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: Quentin Tarantino and Roger Avary's win, same rundown.

films.title: Fargo
films.note: The Coen brothers' win, one of the rare times this category's winner also took Best Picture.

films.title: Good Will Hunting
films.note: Ben Affleck and Matt Damon's famous win, same rundown.

films.title: Gosford Park
films.note: Julian Fellowes's win, same rundown.

films.title: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
films.note: Charlie Kaufman's Oscar, same rundown.

films.title: The Hurt Locker
films.note: Listed among the category's past honorees without further comment.

films.title: Django Unchained
films.note: Same.

films.title: Her
films.note: Same.

films.title: Shakespeare in Love
films.note: The other rare case, with Fargo, of an Original Screenplay winner that also took Best Picture.

segments.heading: The Big Race: Best Adapted Screenplay

films.title: The Ballad of Buster Scruggs
films.note: The Coens' anthology film qualifies here on a technicality: four of its six segments are wholly original, only two based on short stories (Jack London's "All Gold Canyon" and Stewart Edward White's "The Gal Who Got Rattled").

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Spike Lee, David Rabinowitz, Charlie Wachtel, and Kevin Willmott's script, based on Ron Stallworth's memoir; the hosts peg it the frontrunner, mostly as a make-good for Lee's career.

films.title: Can You Ever Forgive Me?
films.note: Nicole Holofcener and Jeff Whitty's script, based on Lee Israel's memoir; Amanda says Holofcener's body of work belongs in the Smithsonian.

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: Barry Jenkins's script, based on James Baldwin's novel; the hosts wonder if it's getting overshadowed because Jenkins already won this category for Moonlight.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Bradley Cooper, Will Fetters, and Eric Roth's script, credited to the 1954 and 1976 films before it. Amanda calls it "a stew of three previous films" that still counts as a real adaptation.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Jenkins and Tarell Alvin McCraney's prior Adapted Screenplay win, the reason the hosts think Beale Street isn't getting more attention here.

films.title: Eighth Grade
films.note: Not an Oscar nominee, but nominated for Original Screenplay at the Writers Guild Awards, which run on different eligibility rules.

films.title: A Quiet Place
films.note: Same WGA Original Screenplay nod; Sean wants to see the shooting script given how little dialogue the film has.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Nominated for Adapted Screenplay at the WGA, not the Oscars; the hosts wonder if older Oscar voters are simply less familiar with Ryan Coogler's work.

segments.heading: In Conversation
segments.note: the Beale Street and Moonlight composer on scoring, Succession, and the pipe organ

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: Britell's second Oscar nomination, after Moonlight; he and Barry Jenkins built the score around the sound of cellos to represent the film's several kinds of love.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Britell's first Oscar nomination; he later published three pieces from the score, including "Little's Theme" and "Middle of the World."

films.title: Chariots of Fire
films.note: Vangelis's score is Britell's earliest film-music memory: he saw the film at five and asked for piano lessons because of it.

films.title: Ghostbusters
films.note: Elmer Bernstein's score, which Britell says he loved as a kid as much as Mozart's Jupiter Symphony.

films.title: Beetlejuice
films.note: Danny Elfman's score, which Britell cites in his running complaint that most film scores never get their sheet music published.

films.title: The Big Short
films.note: Where Britell first met Adam McKay, while it was shooting.

films.title: Gimme the Loot
films.note: Adam Leon's feature debut and Britell's first scored indie feature to get real distribution: it played Cannes and won at SXSW.

films.title: Domino One
films.note: A $10,000 feature his college friend Nick Lavelle made, the first film Britell ever scored; it never got released.

films.title: Eighth Grade
films.note: Britell singles out Anna Meredith's score as one he admires, "so woven into that universe" that he couldn't imagine the film without it.

films.title: Roma
films.note: Britell's answer to the show's closing question about the last great thing he's seen; he met Alfonso Cuarón the same day and says he was "just stammering."

films.title: Children of Men
films.note: The Cuarón film Britell told Cuarón he thinks about every day, on meeting him.

The Man Who Killed Hitler and Then the B.title: The Man Who Killed Hitler and Then the Bigfoot

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind.title: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
